data.title: XLF: Slow Down With The Euphoria
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend holding The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLF). With strong momentum, American banks are the largest in the world, and there are high expectations for their results, especially in Q4.

However, financials is the sector with the largest number of small caps in the S&P 500. The curious fact is that the highest growth expectations are in these same small caps, and many of them are not yet profitable. This raises doubts about the sustainability of the prices, especially with the current valuation.

The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF

The Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF is a well-known ETF among investors that seeks to replicate the Financial Select Sector Index. Below are the ETF's main holdings.

As we can see, the ETF has strong exposure to large American banks, investment holding companies and other companies in the financial sector. Finally, let's get into the investment thesis.

American Banks Are The Largest In The World

When it comes to the banking sector, the dominant countries with the most valuable assets are China and the United States. However, recent measures by China that have scared off investors have given the American financial sector an even greater shine.

Additionally, the United States has been experiencing a great moment in its stock market, and the financial sector is one of the highlights. As we can see, there are high expectations for the results of companies in the financial segment in 4Q24.

Apparently the outlook is very positive, right? However, some data left me quite skeptical about this thesis. Let's analyze each of these data below.

See The Number Of Small Caps In The Financial Segment

When we talk about the American financial sector, we usually think of large banks, characterized by their financial solidity. But let's look at the interesting data in the graph below.

It is interesting to note that the financial segment is the one with the most small caps in its composition among all sectors of the American economy. In my view, small caps are characterized by higher risks, which is not exactly in line with investors who want greater financial solidity.

What Are The Risks Of Greater Exposure To Small Caps?

We can see from the graph below that small caps logically have greater potential for profit growth compared to other companies. However, small caps are the segment with the most unprofitable companies.

I confess that the data presented made me feel a little less confident about the investment thesis in the American financial segment. However, this became even clearer when analyzing the valuation.

Valuation Is Not Attractive

A good method for evaluating a financial ETF is to perform a comparative analysis of multiples against its own history using the P/E. At its lowest point, the ETF traded at 11.4x earnings, and at its highest point 17.3x earnings, for an average of 14.3 P/E.

Currently, the ETF's P/E is 17.2x, significantly above the historical average, which makes me skeptical about the investment thesis and supports the recommendation to hold the asset. But why not make a sell recommendation? We will see this in the risks.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

In response to the question in the previous paragraph, the recommendation is not to sell due to the momentum the ETF is experiencing. Additionally, one of the risks to the thesis is that it is not a consensus, since Seeking Alpha's Quant tools point to a buy recommendation.

Another risk to the thesis is that, although the ETF represents the financial segment as a whole, it is not possible to state that there are 18% of small caps in this ETF as the data shows, nor that these small caps are unprofitable.

So, the data does not necessarily apply 100% to the XLF thesis, but it is certainly a great proxy. The thesis is complex, and investors should conduct diligent analysis of the XLF ETF.

The Bottom Line

The US financial sector has been standing out with strong momentum amid a very bullish market. This can also be seen in the earnings expectations of companies in the sector in 4Q24.

However, we see that the financial segment is the most exposed to small caps (many of which are unprofitable), companies that are not exactly known for their financial solidity, especially with such a high valuation.

Based on this analysis, I recommend holding the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF. In my view, the asset is not that solid in terms of financial fundamentals, especially when we analyze the stretched valuation. The risk/return ratio does not seem attractive to me.

data.title: China's Economic Struggles: Why Cutting Rates Isn't Enough To Boost Steel Demand
data.description:
Original Post

By Sohrab Darabshaw

No matter the type or amount of stimuli put in by the Chinese government, the country's economy is just not getting back into revival mode. Meanwhile, China's beleaguered steel industry continues to suffer. As part of efforts to boost economic growth, China cut down its benchmark lending rates after the apex bank lowered interest rates in late September. Still, according to the latest reports, the expected boost is simply not happening.

The World Steel Association has downwardly revised its earlier 2024 steel demand forecast for most major economies, including China. However, what the WSA says about the latter is by far the most interesting.

Continued weakness in manufacturing activities and refusal by the country's consumers to buy new real estate, among other factors, have all contributed to the downward revision. In fact, the WSA now predicts that the steel demand outlook in China will be less than half of global steel consumption, something that has not happened in the past six years.

Could More Stimulus Aid China's Steel Industry?

The WSA report also stated that the present downturn in real estate will impact steel demand in China, resulting in a 3.0% decline in 2024 and a drop of roughly 1.0% next year. However, the report does not negate the possibility of even more intervention on the government's part to push the economy upward. Depending on the nature of the efforts, they could potentially boost Chinese steel demand in 2025.

China has been trying its best to prop up its economy, which started floundering post-COVID. Recently, China's housing ministry announced an expansion of the "whitelist" of real estate projects as well as measures to help speed up the lending by financial institutions for incomplete projects. What's more, about 50 cities across China have already implemented policies intended to stimulate the real estate market. These include lifting previously imposed restrictions on home buying and even extending offers to non-local buyers.

Reasons Why Measures Have Failed

Since the COVID epidemic, China's economy has faced a slew of ongoing challenges, including a slowing real estate market and weaker consumer demand. The government's counter-response, economists say, is simply not working. Many blame this on remaining uncertainties in global economic conditions, persisting domestic financial risks, and a host of internal Chinese problems.

For instance, China's consumer confidence remains low due to massive youth unemployment. There's also a weakening global demand for exports amid a growing number of geopolitical tensions. Many countries, including neighboring India, continue to protest the flooding of their local markets with cheaper Made In China steel. Others have already imposed tariffs.

Currently, the WSA projects that China's steel consumption will drop to 869 million tons in 2024. In contrast, demand outside of China is expected to increase by 1.2%, reaching 882 million tons.

Is India the New Steelmaking Favorite?

It seems China's primary competitor, India, is the "new steel player in town." The WSA predicts India's market will grow by 8% in 2024. This comes after the local steel industry touched about 14% last year. Along with India, some emerging and developing economies will also see an increase of about 7% for the second year in a row.

The WSA report details how India has emerged as the strongest driver of steel demand growth since 2021. Most importantly, unlike China, major infrastructure projects on the subcontinent will continue to fuel the need for more steel.

As India slowly starts to replace China in steel consumption, the clamor for more tariffs on Chinese steel continues. According to some media reports, India's steel ministry is pushing for a temporary tax on imports to shield the domestic steel industry from the ongoing influx of foreign material, which can lead to price fluctuations and competitive pressures. This tax would serve as a short-term solution to stabilize the market and create a fair environment for domestic producers.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fiserv: No Longer A Bargain But Still A Fairly Valued High-Quality Company
data.description:
Introduction

Fiserv (NYSE:FI) was the first stock I ever covered at Seeking Alpha, and I kept a consistent buy rating all the time based on the fact that the stock was seriously undervalued. In my first article, I had over 50% upside from $100 back then. Most recently, I updated my view in February and still kept it at a buy with a price target of $180 (from $150 at the time of the article). Fast-forward, and we now trade close to $200, which is slightly above my prior price target after rolling it forward for 9 months. And for the first time, I find Fiserv to be fairly valued. What does this mean for my recommendation?

In this article, I will first review Q3 results and their updated outlook. I will then look at how this impacts my DCF valuation. Finally, I will discuss my view on Fiserv now, that the stark undervaluation is gone.

I will assume some basic knowledge about the company and its business. If you want to read up on this, I suggest my initiation article for this.

Fiserv Q3 Results

Fiserv just reported Q3 results. While revenue was a touch weaker (+7%) than expectations (although fully in line with their guidance) they once again beat adjusted EPS targets thanks to higher margins and buybacks. Clover continued to perform strongly with 28% growth. Buybacks in the quarter and YTD continue to be strong, well, in excess of their FCF for the year. One negative development was a non-cash impairment charge of $610m, mainly relating to a Wells Fargo Merchant Services JV. This goes to show that not everything they do necessarily lives up to expectations. Argentina also impacted them negatively, as FX movements were stronger than their benefit from excess inflation. For a more in-depth discussion about the impact of Argentina on their results, please refer to my prior article in February.

Fiserv nevertheless raised their full year adjusted EPS guidance as well as their margin expansion target. They already increased their free cash flow guidance at half year and confirmed this.

Overall, I would say this quarter as well as their performance YTD shows that they are right on track. Revenue growth is maybe slightly weak, impacted by worse FX in Argentina, but better than expected margin expansion makes more than up for it. FCF generation is progressing well, and they did a lot of buybacks, pushing per share statistics.

Updating My Fiserv Valuation

Back in February, my DCF model showed a fair value of $174. Since then, we had three more quarters of results and passage of time. Rolling forward, the present value alone brings me up to $187.

Where do I need to adjust my DCF? They raised their FCF guidance for this year somewhat to $4.7bn and gave us guidance for their capex of $1.5bn p.a. That said, in Q3 their trailing twelve-month capex was still $1524m and running well above 2023. For Q1-3 2024 capex was $1170m. I am therefore sceptical if they are truly able to reduce capex to 1.5bn. As such, I do lower my Capex assumption in my model to 8% of revenue from 8.5% which gives a minor uplift to fair value but does not go for the full $1.5bn target.

As for FCF, I am already in line with their target after making some adjustments. I do regard stock-based compensation as a real expense. Although it is admittedly non-cash, the dilution needs to be canceled, and they do spend real cash on their buybacks. I therefore deduct stock-based compensation from their FCF. In addition, Fiserv adds merger integration and severance costs back to FCF. For a serial acquirer like Fiserv, I do regard these as ongoing costs that will regularly occur. Thus, I do not add them back. That gets me to an FCF of $4.1-4.2bn for 2024 leading to a roughly 27x FCF multiple for 2024.

Margins are slightly better than what I expected but 7% expected revenue growth is a touch lighter than my assumption of 7.5%. Overall, however, my DCF stays fairly stable, and it gives a minor uplift in fair value to $195 after taking into account the reduced number of shares and the passage of time:

Which is pretty much the current share price. Checking this with FAST Graphs shows a similar result:

This chart goes from 2019 as that was the date of the first data merger, which was a transformational merger that strongly improved Fiserv's growth profile. Looking further back would even show an overvaluation, but I do not consider this adequate.

Therefore, Fiserv now, for the first time since I covered the stock, is trading around fair value. How does that impact my thinking about the stock and my rating?

The Importance of a Margin of Safety

Benjamin Graham coined the term "margin of safety" for investing, and his disciple Warren Buffett further popularized it. Simply put, it was an approach to limit losses. Investing in stocks and trying to determine the fair value of a security is no easy task. The fair value of a stock depends on the unknown future cash flows of the company. One might try to estimate these, but in the end, the future is unknown.

Some of those uncertainties come from within the company and the competitive environment. But others come from very different parts of society, like regulations, court decisions or tax rules. It is impossible to forecast all of these parts correctly, and therefore any fair value calculation is only the best guess. And that is not even counting errors of judgment made by the analyst himself.

And this is not just a theoretical exercise. Just take the example of taxes. It is obvious that the future tax rate will impact the cash flows that are left over for shareholders. Corporate tax rates were 35% not that long ago and are currently 21% in the US. Kamala Harris has now announced her intention to raise the tax rate to 28%. Whether this will ever happen is dependent on both the highly uncertain presidential election outcome and the results of Congress. But even if those would be in favor for her, it still does not mean the tax changes will truly be realized. But if it were, that would mean that of every dollar of earnings only 72c would accrue to shareholders compared to 79c right now. This will obviously impact the valuation of a company and should lower it by nearly 9%. Even Warren Buffett has indicated that he sold Apple due to his expectation of higher taxes going forward simply because of the ever-increasing fiscal deficit, and that was well before Kamala Harris made her statement.

Therefore, if you buy a stock without a margin of safety you could find yourself quickly in a position where you actually overpaid for it, and you will earn sub-par returns. Investing with a margin of safety tries to reduce the probability of this happening. It is thus an approach to limit your downside risk. But what about the upside?

Fiserv - a wonderful business at a fair price?

In his highly discussed paper "Do Stocks Outperform Treasury Bills?" Prof. Hendrik Bessembinder showed that the vast majority of gains from the US stock market came from only a few selected stocks:

Four out of every seven common stocks that have appeared in the CRSP database since 1926 have lifetime buy-and-hold returns less than one-month Treasuries. When stated in terms of lifetime dollar wealth creation, the best-performing four percent of listed companies explain the net gain for the entire U.S. stock market since 1926, as other stocks collectively matched Treasury bills.

4 out of every 7 stocks underperform Treasury bills. And only 4% of all stocks account for all the outperformance of the stock market versus US Treasuries. And it is certainly something that I can attest to from my personal experience as well: The best performing stocks in my portfolio do really drive the overall returns!

Charlie Munger famously advised Warren Buffett that he should rather buy wonderful businesses at fair prices than try to buy fair businesses at wonderful prices. A Forbes article quotes him on the subject:

Over the long term, it's hard for a stock to earn a much better return than the business which underlies it earns. If the business earns 6% on capital over 40 years and you hold it for that 40 years, you're not going to make much different than a 6% return-even if you originally buy it at a huge discount." (Charlie Munger)

The lesson here was that once you find a wonderful company - and those companies are rare, as Bessembinder's research shows - you better hold on to it. Again, from the Forbes article:

This is why Munger favored very long holding periods, once he found something worth investing in.

This is the counterpoint to the "margin of safety" concept. Care less about the initial valuation and more about the long-term compounding potential inherent in the company. And I tend to agree. I have held on to companies that I considered great even through some periods of seeming overvaluation and have been rewarded. But then there are also others where I wish I sold them (or more) when they were clearly overvalued.

All of this obviously begs the question of whether Fiserv is a truly wonderful company. Should one sell now that the undervaluation is gone, should one hold on or even buy at still fair prices?

Let's first take a look at its FAST Graphs without the price:

This is a picture of adjusted earnings per share growth over the last 20 years. It was never negative and only once dropped below 10%. CAGR was nearly 16%. This is outstanding! A lot of it was driven by regular buybacks pushing up the per share numbers.

Underlying revenue growth looks like this:

As one can see, sales are less smooth, with even twice a negative growth rate. Overall growth was also driven by the First Data Merger, distorting the picture. Here it is also helpful to look at the per share data, although that one is then again impacted by the buybacks:

Revenue and adjusted earnings seem to indicate a well-run business with good growth and high resilience. However, it is worth pointing out that recent revenue growth rates have been lower than in the past. This is not expected to change, with forecasted revenue growth rates being upper single digits rather than double digits.

A large part of that data was before the First Data Merger in 2019. The original Fiserv business was mostly with banks and had a really high moat. The current driver of the shares is, however, the merchant acquirer business that came with First Data, especially Clover. As such, the history is much shorter.

Add to this that the merchant business is much more competitive with many new and innovative start-ups trying to gain market share, and venture capital might once again be willing to fund them after the Fed started easing. On the other hand, Fiserv is trying to lock in customers by constantly adding additional functionality to their offers. This is from their Q3 conference call:

First, we announced this morning with DoorDash, we began implementing a comprehensive embedded finance application poised to be a best-in-class solution for gig economy company. This solution enables DoorDash to offer its delivery contractors a full range of financial services. Within a single app, they can get instant access to wages and rewards, as well as deposit accounts, debit cards and more.

Furthermore, Fiserv gets a lot of its growth now from entry into international markets. This is also more risky compared to serving US banks. Just look at the situation in Argentina, where inflation is triple digit and the country is in a recession. In Europe, the ECB plans to introduce a digital Euro as a means for electronic payment.

Fiserv has always been a deal making company, and I regard this approach as inherently more risky than organic growth.

More recently, there have also been reports by unhappy customers about Clover.

Clover POS reviews are very mixed. The negative Clover reviews can be concerning since many of them allege a lack of transparency from the company and Clover merchant services (provided by parent company Fiserv) in particular.

Trustpilot and Capterra both had recently very poor reviews for Clover. I do not want to overly emphasize those as these are just a selection of people with poor experiences and overall, Clover often gets high praise. But there might be a gap between what Clover intends to be and the actual customer experience. The issues raised by them could point towards larger problems at Clover and whether Fiserv is able to handle this kind of growth in a customer focussed business. It is certainly something to watch. As the usual lock-in term seems to be 48 months, those kinds of issues also might only show up with a time delay.

Overall, I would say that Fiserv might get close to being a wonderful business. But they are still in the process of building out their moat in the merchant business. As of now, this area is continuing to be highly competitive. Once they have added more and more functionality to their solutions, it will get harder and harder for customers to switch. However, I don't think we are there yet.

Also, while electronic payments volumes should be fairly stable, overall revenue growth seems to be stuck in the upper single digits, limiting compounding potential. Together with margin expansion, this should still lead to satisfactory but not necessarily outstanding returns. Add to that regulatory uncertainty, constant deal making and buybacks at maybe soon too high prices, I would argue Fiserv is not yet a truly wonderful business. But pretty good nevertheless.

Verdict

I guess it is obvious by now that I struggle to decide whether to put a "buy" or "hold" on Fiserv. In the end, I believe that calling it a very strong "hold" is the right thing to do. Holding but not actively adding is also what I do personally. The undervaluation is gone. There is currently no margin of safety embedded in the stock price and the market feels overall too complacent, especially with a highly contentious election around the corner. I recently wrote an article about Fiserv's competitor Global Payments (GPN) where my main argument was the very enticing risk-return profile with only limited downside. The same unfortunately cannot be said for Fiserv at this point after its huge run-up, although I believe Fiserv is the higher quality business of the two. Therefore, I do now downgrade Fiserv to a very strong hold as it has finally reached fair value.

If you found this article helpful, please vote for it as a compelling analysis. If you have questions or remarks, please comment below. I would love to hear from you. Many thanks!

data.title: Brown-Forman: This Dividend Aristocrat Hasn't Been This Interesting For Years
data.description:
It is not an easy time for companies that sell spirits, some of which have seen their price per share drop double-digit. This industry as far as I am concerned is very interesting because it is quite concentrated and the profit margins are high.

After writing this article on Pernod Ricard, I became interested in learning more about the companies that are part of this industry and I came across Brown-Forman (NYSE:BF.B). Its brands have a century-long history and its earnings results are improving steadily over the long term, as well as having uncommon profitability.

Since the all-time high ($83.01, Dec 14, 2020), the price per share has fallen 42%, which in my view offers a very attractive buying opportunity given that the underlying business remains solid. Certainly there are challenges in the short term, but I am optimistic about its long-term future.

Challenges to be faced

The stock's performance in recent years has been very disappointing, and that is a fact: -24% in the last 5 years in the midst of a bullish market.

The slump has become more pronounced over the past few quarters, and there have been a several factors that have caused it. First, its main brand, Jack Daniel's, is not performing as well as in the past.

In the last quarter its net sales fell by 8% or 6% on an organic basis. Since the Jack Daniel's brand is fundamental to the whole company, if its sales drop then the market starts to panic. Anyway, the reasons behind why sales have not gone up do not depend on a deterioration of the brand, simply on some complications related to a change in ordering patterns.

In the past year, U.S. companies anticipated orders to get ahead of the pre-announced price increase; same thing in the U.K., but to save on the rise in excise taxes. The result has been increased inventory this year, with negative consequences for both sales and cash flow from operations. In addition, the divestments of Finland and Sonoma-Cutrer were another factor affecting the decline in sales and gross margin.

Compared to Q1 FY2024, during the last quarterly period net sales dropped almost everywhere, but what has been said so far should be taken into consideration. In my opinion, these are issues that are temporary in nature and do not challenge the company's long-term growth. Certainly, there are countries where its brands have a brighter future than others.

In the U.S., the decline in sales also occurred last year, but in emerging markets, growth has been significant. In a normalized context, I expect the trend to be more or less this, i.e., improvement in developing countries and consolidated demand in developed countries. After all, everyone in Europe and the U.S. knows Jack Daniel's and it is not easy to improve market share; even just consolidating it would be a great achievement.

All of these are tangible problems, but I don't think they are the main reasons why the stock has lost up to 50%: the market is probably worried about tariffs.

Emerging markets are critical to BF's long-term growth, but in recent months trade wars are causing it quite a few headaches. Recently, after the European Union increased tariffs on imported Chinese vehicles, China in turn increased them toward brandy imports from the European Union. BF is not affected in this situation, but it may be affected in some way in the future since China-U.S. trade relations are not the best. Who will be the new U.S. president will count a lot in this respect.

But tensions are not only between U.S. and China, but also between U.S. and Europe. In late 2023 the Old Continent had decided to impose a 50% tax on U.S. whiskey after there were tensions over steel and aluminum trade. Fortunately, after a short time this tariff was suspended, but it could go into effect as of March 31, 2025. Of course, as the market is forward-looking, in BF's current price per share it is already discounting that this tariff will materialize, albeit a few quarters in advance.

Overall, inventory issues and potential new tariffs are the short-term challenges BF has to overcome, and this is what the market is most concerned about. In a sense, if the tariffs were to be permanent, this would no longer be a short-term issue. The hope of course is that geopolitical tensions will ease, at least between the U.S. and Europe.

Secular history and future prospects

We have seen how poor performance has been over the past 5 years, and short-term difficulties could sink the company even more. But then why am I optimistic? The reason lies mainly in the history of this company.

When you invest in a sin stock, regulatory risk is always around the corner; the same happens with companies that sell tobacco. Taxes, tariffs, and litigation are routine, but that is the price you pay for operating in such a profitable industry with few peers.

Take a look at BF's results from 2002 to the present: there have been ups and downs, but in the long run it has always improved. Do not give too much weight to the last two years, as they are affected by the growing inventory for the reasons mentioned above. The same goes for profit margins and return on capital.

This does not mean that BF is destined to grow forever, but that the introduction (by the way, not yet certain) of the 50% tariff in Europe, and the potential ones in China will not put an end to a company with 150 years of experience: it will take more than that.

Throughout its history BF has several times seen its price plummet by 50%, but in the end it has always found its way to reborn stronger than before. We can expect some short-term difficulties with profitability, but it will still remain very high for a consumer staple company. Its huge economies of scale combined with the strength of its brands is what makes me most optimistic.

Although the market is punishing BF, management's guidance has remained unchanged for FY2025.

Forget the crisis, organic growth in operating income is expected to be 2-4%.

Dividend and valuation

For a company whose price per share has plummeted as much as 50%, I expect that its EPS has been deteriorating for years and that the dividend is becoming unsustainable, but this description is exactly the opposite of what is happening to BF.

BF is a dividend aristocrats close to becoming a dividend king since it has issued growing dividends for 40 years in a row. If we consider all dividends excluding growth, BF has been issuing one every quarter for 80 years.

Even before many of us were born this company was already issuing a growing dividend, and since its EPS shows excellent long-term growth, I expect this will not change for a few decades. Unlike a tech company that has to continuously innovate to keep up with the competition, BF simply has to keep doing what it has been doing so far, occasionally juggling short-term issues. Its financial results show a clear competitive advantage, as well as a consistency that can be found in few companies. The only drawback is that the dividend yield is quite low, 1.80%, but its growth is faster than the median for the consumer staples sector.

By investing in BF you should not expect to get rich in a short time, but to consolidate your capital in a solid company with predictable revenues and capable of generating a low but highly probable passive income. I don't want to get too out of line about the sustainability of the dividend (we never have certainty), but a payout ratio of less than 50% (except 2023) over the last 22 years is something not very common.

BF is a company for the risk-averse, but do not underestimate the potential of its capital gains.

Do not rely on appearances because if you compare its valuation ratios with those of peers you would think it is extremely overvalued. The point is that few consumer staples companies have such high profit margins, which is why I justify higher multiples for BF.

If you compare BF's profitability with its peers, you will see that this company has an edge.

In terms of net income margin and levered FCF margin no one can compete; regarding Return on Equity Diageo (DEO) takes the first place. Yet, as we saw in the first image of the article, BF has been among the companies whose price per share has fallen the most since the beginning of the year.

In my view, the market has overly punished this company, whose financial performance remains of high quality; the recent collapse makes it an interesting buying opportunity that has not happened in a long time.

Take a look at the recent NTM Total Enterprise Value / EBIT, it is currently 21.20x, but the 5-year average is 26.94x. Basically, BF has never been this cheap except during the peak of the pandemic. Buying it a few weeks ago would have been more appropriate, but at the current price it remains a buy.

Conclusion

Brown-Forman is a leading company in its industry and its brands have a centuries-old history. It is facing a complicated period because of inventory issues and potential tariffs; the latter would have a major impact on foreign sales.

Personally, I remain optimistic given BF's experience in dealing with short-term issues, and I do not believe the end has come for this dividend behemoth. Despite the troubles, its financial results are still strong and its profitability remains among the best in its industry.

I find such a large slump unjustified, in fact, its NTM Total Enterprise Value / EBIT is on the lows of the last 5 years. As for me, I have opened a small, long position on this company, with the intention of increasing my investment if the price per share continues to collapse. My next price target is the $42 per share and at that point the rating could change to strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Enphase Energy: More Pain Ahead
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bearish thesis about Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH) aged well as the stock significantly lost its value since July 3. Price at publication was $97, around 20% higher than we see today after the Q3 earnings release. It was a disappointing quarter for investors. Despite some small signs of recovery, macro headwinds remain quite strong which we can see from the cautious Q4 guidance from the management. Another big issue is that the stock is still notably overvalued, according to my DCF model. All in all, I reiterate a "Strong Sell" rating for ENPH.

Recent developments

The company has just reported its Q3 earnings, which are quite disappointing. Revenue of $380.9 million dropped by around 31% and was $13 million below consensus. Revenue miss is quite wide and so is the bottom-line negative surprise. The Q3 adjusted EPS of $0.65 missed consensus estimates by $0.13. The adjusted EPS was $1.02 in Q3 2023, which is significantly higher as well.

The management's expectations around the next quarter are quite cautious, which adds to the bearish sentiment around the stock. Revenue is expected to be within a range of $360 million to $400 million, which includes shipments of 140 to 160 megawatt hours of IQ Batteries. The midpoint of the projected revenue range is $380 million, which will be a 25% YoY increase. While such a topline increase might look positive, the Q4 revenue guidance is much lower than $433 million expected by Wall Street analysts.

The balance sheet appears solid with a secure $256 million cash position and $1.5 billion in highly liquid securities. Inventory levels have decreased notably compared to December 31, 2023, but they remain quite high. For instance, the company's Q3 2021 revenue was approximately 10% lower at $351 million, yet the ending inventory balance was only $65 million, more than twice as low compared to the latest reporting date. Therefore, while the trend in inventory levels is positive, there is still significant work to be done to make the outstanding balance more comfortable compared to historical levels when the business was thriving. This is a temporary yet notable problem, as ENPH will need to sell off its inventory before returning to its historical profitability levels. And this process can take a few quarters because the demand still looks quite volatile.

Analyzing Enphase's Q3 earnings call left me with more bearish than bullish thoughts as well. International expansion potential was one of the strong reasons why there were so many ENPH optimists. However, in Q3 revenue in Europe was down by 15% on a sequential basis with a 34% decrease in a sell-through. This indicates that the environment is still challenging for international expansion, which might undermine the company's long-term revenue growth potential.

The U.S. market demonstrated strength in Q3 with a solid 43% QoQ revenue growth, but the situation is not flawless here as well. According to the management, one of the company's largest U.S. customers went bankrupt, which indicates that the environment in the U.S. is still quite challenging. Since ENPH historically differentiated itself with superior customer service, the fact that average call wait times increased in Q3 also looks quite negative.

The fact that the Fed started cutting interest rates in September is likely a tailwind for ENPH, but it is also crucial to understand the context. The U.S. economic growth is expected to slow in 2025, which directly affects prices for hydrocarbons. As a result, in the above table we see that crude oil prices are expected to decline in 2025, and there was a notable downgrade compared to EIA's previous forecasts. Cheaper alternative to solar energy is certainly a headwind for ENPH.

Valuation update

The stock price declined by 4% over the last 12 months, which is notably behind the broader U.S. market. On the other hand, performance in 2024 is even weaker, with a 30% YTD share price drop. ENPH's valuation ratios are still sky-high, which we can see from a very low "D-" valuation grade from Seeking Alpha Quant.

Looking only at valuation ratios is never enough for me, so I proceed with the DCF model. I start with figuring out the cost of equity, which will be the discount rate. The approach used to calculate ENPH's cost of equity is the capital asset pricing model. All variables are easily available on the internet, and ENPH's cost of equity is 13.7%.

I always prefer my DCF model to be kind of contrarian to my thesis. Therefore, if I am more bearish about the stock, then I try to use more aggressive assumptions for the DCF. Consensus estimates project ENPH's revenue CAGR for the next decade to be 16.5%. I am using a more aggressive 18% revenue CAGR to be contrarian to my bearish stance. The base-year FCF margin assumption is 15.15%, which is the past five years' average. Due to ENPH's historical ability to drive profitability growth, I incorporate a 50 basis point yearly FCF margin expansion.

As we see above, even with extremely aggressive assumptions, the business's fair value is still 11% lower than the market cap. If I incorporate a 16.5% revenue CAGR in line with consensus, the company's fair value drops below $10 billion.

Therefore, I do not find ENPH's valuation attractive. I think that it will be much more attractive if the market cap drops closer to $9 billion, in case oil prices do not plunge unexpectedly.

Risks to my bearish thesis

From the chart below, we can see that revenue growth was high during 2022, coinciding with a spike in oil prices following the onset of the Russia-Ukraine war. This is logical, as customers tend to seek cheaper alternatives when traditional energy becomes more expensive. Therefore, if there is another sudden shock in the hydrocarbons market, ENPH could benefit from the situation. Since oil-rich countries like Russia and Iran are still involved in direct military conflicts, there is always a risk of strikes impacting oil infrastructure, which would likely lead to panic in energy markets and cause oil prices to soar.

We examined Enphase's balance sheet earlier in the article and noted that the company boasts ample liquidity. This makes Enphase financially strong enough to invest aggressively in innovation, which means there is always the potential to roll out a revolutionary product that could become a bestseller. According to the earnings release, during Q3, the company launched AI-based software designed to optimize energy use by integrating solar and consumption forecasting with electricity tariffs. Since optimizing energy use results in lower costs for customers, this feature likely enhances Enphase's value proposition. Moreover, there are also some other promising products in the pipeline like fourth-generation energy system, featuring the IQ Meter Collar, 10 kWh IQ Battery, and enhanced IQ Combiner, which are expected to debut in the next few years.

Bottom line

Despite Enphase's firm commitment to innovation, I still consider it a "Strong Sell". Wide earnings miss and a notable Q4 guidance downgrade are big red flags indicating that industry headwinds are still quite strong. Moreover, the stock looks notably overvalued at current levels.

data.title: Fading Rate Cut Expectations Push U.S. Dollar Even Higher
data.description:
A labor market that refuses to cool, inflation that remains stable and an economy that continues to expand have lowered expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates much this year and bolstered the US dollar.

Since the end of September, the US dollar index, which measures the dollar against a basket of foreign currencies, has risen 3.4% as inflation and jobs data have lowered the odds for multiple rate cuts in the last two Fed meetings of 2024.

The dollar has surged against its foreign peers since late September after the Fed cut rates for the first time since cutting them to near zero at the start of the pandemic in 2020. The rising probability that the Fed's first rate cut this year could prove to be its last as the domestic economy continues to thrive will likely bolster the dollar further.

"The US dollar has already strengthened this month on the back of the view that the US economy remains more resilient than expected," said Jane Foley, head of foreign exchange strategy at Rabobank. "If the jobs market remains relatively robust and the Fed cuts rates less than expected, US dollar strength would almost certainly be extended."

As of Oct. 21, about one-third of the futures market was expecting the Fed to cut its benchmark rate by just 25 basis points by the end of December, meaning the Fed would have at least one meeting where officials would not cut at all, according to the CME FedWatch Tool, which measures investor sentiment in the Fed funds futures market. A month earlier, the majority of the market was betting that the Fed would cut at least 75 bps before the end of this year. Now, no one is betting on cuts of that size, according to the tool.

The ongoing US dollar rally would get "turbocharged" if the Fed's monetary policy easing cycle is more moderate than any other foreign central bank's cutting cycle, said Elias Haddad, a senior markets strategist with Brown Brothers Harriman.

The dollar tends to rise with interest rates and tighter monetary conditions as investors move to government bonds and other dollar-denominated securities. A stronger dollar bolsters US spending power abroad but can dent overseas profits for US-based companies.

As expectations for the start of the Fed's rate cut cycle ramped up this summer, the dollar lost ground to all its G10 peers. But since the first cut at the Fed's September meeting, the dollar has gained on all of them. If the Fed continues to appear less dovish, the dollar will likely continue to rally against these foreign currencies, Foley with Rabobank said.

The Norwegian krone and the Japanese yen could fare the worst if the Fed is slow to cut rates, said Derek Halpenny, head of research for global markets at MUFG.

Outside of rates, Halpenny said he sees the US election as the primary other issue driving the dollar. Some of the rally in the dollar is likely due to expectations of a Donald Trump victory in November, since many of his policy plans are likely to curb any Fed easing plans.

"Tariffs, fiscal expansion and mass deportations are all inflationary and that will see the Fed doing less and drive the dollar stronger initially at least," Halpenny said. "I am not sure if over the medium term Trump will be good for the dollar, but initially through the remainder of 2024 and over the first half of 2025 it probably will."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Floor & Decor's Expansion Plans Weighing On Profits
data.description:
Floor & Decor Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:FND) is bound to release Q3 2024 earnings on October 30.

The news section of the company's website shows several new opening stores since the release of Q2 earnings.

However, it seems that the strategic initiative to expand operations across the US by opening new stores is pressuring the profits of the company.

With a double-digit decline in operating and net income, the company is struggling to improve its profitability due to the increase in expenses associated with the opening of new stores.

In this article, I considered reviewing this, and other factors that led to my Hold rating, including a significant sell transaction by one of the activist investors.

For now, I would like to begin with a brief company overview section.

Company Overview

Floor & Decor is a retailer that specializes in selling hard surface flooring products, including tile, wood, laminate, vinyl, and natural stone, as well as accessories and tools needed for installation.

They operate both physical stores and an online platform, with 230 stores across 36 states as of mid-2024.

Their strategy is to keep a wide variety of flooring products in stock at low prices. The company targets both homeowners and professionals in the construction and renovation industries.

I considered including below a breakdown of their revenue per product type from the latest 10Q.

Ownership-wise, the latest 14A shows 2.1% ownership among all 16 directors and executive officers. In my view, not enough skin in the game.

Price Action

A quick look at the daily chart below shows a positive long-term trend, although the stock shows periods of high volatility.

Over the last 5 years, the stock has outperformed the home improvement retail industry, the consumer cyclical sector, and the S&P 500.

The decline in 2022 reflects how sensitive this stock is to a downturn in the broader markets.

Fundamentals

The income statement shows a stagnation in revenue growth, with a 0.4% TTM decline.

However, what really concerns me is the double-digit decline in operating income (-32.3% YoY) and net income (-27.3%).

Let's dive deeper into the latest annual report to understand the drivers behind this decline.

It seems that the sharp increase in operating expenses played a fundamental role. Total operating expenses increased by 15.5% in 2023, largely due to increases in selling and store operating expenses (up 14.9%) and general and administrative expenses (up 18.2%).

This increase was mainly related to new store openings and additional costs related to Spartan's expansion into new markets.

To give some context, the company opened 31 new warehouse-style stores in fiscal 2023 and 9 more in 2024 (as of June 27, 2024).

Another factor to consider is the decline in comparable store sales, with a 7.1% drop in 2023, and a 10.3% decline in H1 2024.

Considering the nature of its business, the company's performance is closely tied to new home construction and existing home sales.

My long-term outlook on new home construction in the US is cautiously optimistic considering falling material costs, like lumber, which has decreased significantly from its peak in 2021, and the high possibility of interest rates gradually decreasing in 2025.

As a side note, the September 2024 US residential construction data shows housing completions increasing by 14.6% YoY, while housing starts in September have been relatively stable compared to the same month last year.

Moving on to the balance sheet, net debt has decreased since the peak in 2022, sitting at $1.55 billion at the end of last quarter.

The company is accumulating cash reserves, which added up to $138.1 million at the end of last quarter, up from $34.4 million at the end of 2023.

The liquidity ratios look ok, considering the company's high inventory levels.

Liquidity Ratio Value
Current Ratio 1.17
Quick Ratio 0.27
Cash Ratio 0.12

As an additional note, the quick ratio seems low, however, it has been improving over the past years.

This ratio is still low compared to some of the company's industry peers (see last column of the table below).

Moving on to the cash flow statement, if we consider a yearly timeframe, there seems to be a significant improvement in operating and free cash flows since 2022.

However, looking at a quarterly timeframe, the picture is quite different, with a constant YoY decline since Q4 2023.

The contributing factors in Q2 were a $19.1 million increase in accounts payable, a $9.8 million increase in accounts receivable, and a significant income tax payment of $24.4 million.

Aside from these factors, funds from operations were lower, due to the increase in operating expenses discussed at the start of this section.

A similar trend is shown in Q1, with a $35.1 million reduction in accounts payable and a 30% decline in net income.

Insider / Activist / Institutional Activity

I remain disappointed with the lack of insider buying activity this year. It seems that most executives are exercising their call options, and some directors are selling shares in the open market.

Additionally, one of the activist investors, Capital World, sold 48% of their ownership at the start of October this year, without any relevant buying activity from other activist investors since February this year.

In the second quarter, institutional investors have been increasing the call options in this stock by 39%, leading to a 30% decline in the put/call ratio.

However, the number of shares owned by 13F filers stayed the same, signaling that most funds are taking a wait and see approach.

Valuation

A quick glance at how the valuation ratios are performing compared to their 5-year average gives a mixed result.

On one hand, P/CF is trading close to the 5-year historical minimum, however, EV/EBITDA is quite close to the maximum of its range.

So, I decided to compare the company's EV/EBITDA with its competitors to get a sense of how it measures up.

The table above was sorted in descending order by EV/EBITDA, showing Floor & Decor being the most expensive company among its peers when compared to this metric.

As a side note, when using P/CF, the company is the 3rd most expensive.

Conclusion

To wrap up, my rating for this company is Hold due to concerns with the sharp increase in operating expenses, mainly driven by aggressive store expansion, leading to a decline in operating and net income.

Additionally, declining comparable store sales further exacerbate the issue, pointing to weakening demand.

Despite some improvements in the balance sheet, such as reduced net debt and a spike in cash reserves, the quick ratio of the company remains a concern, especially when compared to industry peers. The company has high inventory levels, adding up to $1.04 billion at the end of last quarter.

Valuation metrics also suggest the stock is expensive relative to its peers, particularly in terms of EV/EBITDA.

I anticipate that a recovery in profitability will depend on stabilizing expenses, improving comparable sales, and favorable macroeconomic conditions, particularly in the new home construction industry.

data.title: Birkenstock Holding: Undervalued, With Tailwinds Ahead
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my view, Birkenstock Holding plc (NYSE:BIRK) has a lot of tailwinds ahead as it is expanding its business by capitalizing on untapped markets in Asia and continues its sustainability practices. The company also has performed very well in recent years, with great financial growth across many metrics. Furthermore, based on my own DCF analysis, I find that the stock is undervalued considerably by the market. Therefore, I recommend a buy rating on BIRK.

Company Overview

Birkenstock is a German-based footwear company that started in 1774 with the goal of promoting wellness through healthy, high-quality, and comfortable footwear. Birkenstock is in a competitive category of sandal/footwear industry with competitors such as Teva, Chaco, Keen, and many more. Birkenstock offers many types of footwear including sandals, clogs, shoes, sneakers, footwear accessories, and etc. Birkenstock also has an international presence, selling to Europe, Australia, New Zealand, Asia, the Middle East, and the Americas.

Business Drivers

One competitive advantage Birkenstock has is their sustainability, and how Birkenstock uses their sustainability as an advantage to differentiate from competitors. Birkenstock uses very sustainable materials in their footwear, such as cork, leather, canvas, Birko-Flor® and more. Using these materials, Birkenstock can create a durable and sustainable product that can be reused and minimize its global footprint. Mainly within their sandals, Birkenstock has created sandals that are meant to be resoled, reducing waste, increasing longevity, and being cost-effective. Additionally, Birkenstock has ensured their production of footwear is environmentally friendly. Birkenstock has made their factories energy efficient using renewable energy, while also using ecological packaging, having compact packaging, efficiently using space during transport and cutting down on CO2 emissions. This focus on sustainability will push the business forward as demand for sustainable products gain momentum around the world.

Birkenstock has shifted its recent focus to the Asian market. Recently, Birkenstock has started to focus on expanding its presence in the Asian market, and as of Q3 FY 2024, Asia has contributed 11.3% of the total revenue, which is a small portion compared to the Americas and Europe. I believe Asia has large potential to start contributing more revenue to Birkenstock, increasing total revenue and its share. In 2023, Asia accounted for more than half of the world's consumption of footwear, at a large share of 54.7% in 2022, compared to North America’s 13.4% and Europe’s 13.9%. Furthermore, Birkenstock has brought on a new manager to target Asia. In our view, such moves are a good indication of management’s desire to capitalize on the ever large Asian footwear market. I believe Asia will continue to contribute more to the total revenue in the medium term, and accelerating growth there would increase momentum.

Financial Analysis

Based on August 2024 Investor Presentation, the company’s financial performance has been great, showing improvements in key categories. Comparing the EBITDA from YTD Q1-Q3 from FY23 to FY24, the company grew this metric at an 11.11% YoY rate, but more impressive was the company’s large revenue growth of 20.77% YoY rate in the same period from 2023 to 2024. Net Profit and EPS all trended in the right direction, though margin has declined a tad bit. These results demonstrate the company’s ability to grow in a challenged economic environment, and demonstrate a good foundation for the company to work from.

Valuation

I conducted a DCF on Birkenstock to determine the value of the stock. I assumed that the Free Cash Flow would grow on average at 12%, which is half of the company’s cash flow CAGR over the past 3 years. I took on a more conservative approach, and assumed a 12% growth rate for the next 7 years, which seems achievable given the strong business drivers. A terminal growth rate of 3% was used as a proxy for inflation and GDP growth, as well as the view that the company will keep growing as it has done historically. With these assumptions, the model implies an intrinsic value per share of $77.21, which represents a large 52.1% upside from current levels.

Risk From Competition

Birkenstock faces substantial risk from competition, as the company operates in a very competitive footwear market. Their main competitors are Teva, Chaco, and Keen, which all have products similar to Birkenstock. Managing competitive risk is crucial in the apparel/footwear industry as changes in consumer preference and inability to innovate new designs can ruin a brand rather quickly. However, Birkenstock is able to stand out from the competition through their unique sole construction, the use of latex and cork, and their branding/design. Teva, Chaco, and Keen all predominantly use a mix of rubber and polyurethane and EVA foam. This difference separates Birkenstock from their competition, as their soles will mold to the wearers feet. The sole of some products can also be resoled. The difference in sole material helps Birkenstock remain distinct in the new niche of the footwear/sandal market. Thus, Birkenstock’s unique product differentiation mitigates the risks of competition.

Recommendation/Conclusion

With strong tailwinds for the business, I recommend a “BUY” for Birkenstock Holding plc as the valuation presents a 52.1% upside for Birkenstock shares. Though Birkenstock faces stiff competition, I believe these risks are minimal and can be negated through its brand and product differentiation. Additionally, many of Birkenstock’s business drivers are strong, especially as management plans to capitalize on untapped markets in Asia. Birkenstock has large potential, and I will continue to follow this company and update readers based on them.

data.title: UTES: A Backdoor Into The AI Theme
data.description:
Can a Utility-focused fund make you rich? Yes, if you happen to have bought the Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (NYSEARCA:UTES). While researching top-performing funds, I came across the UTES ETF and its performance has been stunning.

The managers at Reaves Asset Management have capitalized on the emerging investment theme of AI data center energy demand, loading UTES portfolio with utility companies that are most likely to benefit. This has powered the UTES ETF to a stunning 57% return in the past year, amongst the best of any fund.

However, the downside of the UTES ETF's strategy is that the fund is now indirectly tied to the fortunes of the AI investment theme, which may be more volatile than what utility investors are traditionally used to.

Personally, I am cautious about the AI investment theme, as I see similarities with the 2000 dot-com bubble, so I would be hesitant to bet on the UTES ETF. However, investors may decide the UTES ETF could be an interesting 'backdoor' to the AI investment theme within the Utilities sector. I rate UTES a hold.

Fund Overview

The Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF is an actively managed Utilities-sector-focused ETF that aims to deliver strong risk-adjusted returns from utility and energy investments. The fund is managed by Reaves Asset Management, an infrastructure and utilities sector expert more well-known for the Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG), a long-running closed-end fund ("CEF").

The UTES ETF is managed using Reaves' disciplined investment process, which actively seeks attractive investment opportunities while adhering to strict risk management reviews (Figure 1). Investment entries and exits are assessed by mapping out catalysts and market conditions, and investment positions are continuously monitored.

The UTES ETF is a relatively small fund with only $229 million in AUM (Figure 2). The fund charges a 0.49% total expense ratio.

Portfolio Holdings

Figure 3 shows the top 10 holdings of the UTES ETF. The largest holding in the UTES ETF is NextEra Energy (NEE) at 15.9% weight, followed by Constellation Energy (CEG) at 13.0%, and Vistra Corp. (VST) at 12.7%. In total, UTES' top 10 holdings comprise 77% of the fund's portfolio, making the UTES ETF a very concentrated fund.

Figure 4 shows the top 10 holdings of the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) for comparison. Relative to XLU, the UTES ETF has a much larger holding in NextEra, Constellation, and Vistra, which combine for 41.6% of the UTES ETF versus 24.3% for the XLU ETF.

Historical Returns

The UTES ETF has had a fantastic run of performance in the past year, returning 45.0% YTD to September 30, 2024 and 56.8% on a 1-year basis. However, longer-term returns have been more modest, with 3- and 5-year average annual returns of 17.9% and 11.3% respectively (Figure 5).

While the UTES ETF's short-term returns are impressive, what really sets the fund apart is its relative performance. In the past year, the UTES ETF has returned 64.3% compared to 46.5% for the passive XLU ETF, as the manager's stock-picking process has delivered nearly 18% of outperformance (Figure 6).

However, a longer-term analysis suggests UTES' outperformance is a recent phenomenon, as the performance of UTES ETF was nearly identical to the XLU ETF prior to 2023 (Figure 7).

What changed in the past year to turbocharge UTES' performance?

Backdoor Way To Play AI Theme

While I turned cautious on the Utilities sector earlier in the year, others, like the managers at Reaves Asset Management, saw an emerging opportunity within the Utilities sector with respect to artificial intelligence's ("AI") energy demands.

According to McKinsey, data center energy consumption is expected to more than double over the coming few years to over 400 GWh (Figure 8).

In fact, energy demand from AI companies is heating up so much that the hyperscalers (those companies with large distributed computing infrastructure) have been striking deals directly with utility companies for energy.

For example, Microsoft (MSFT) recently signed an agreement with Constellation Energy to restart the Three Mile Island nuclear power plant in order to supply the company with carbon-free energy. Similarly, Google's parent, Alphabet (GOOGL), has recently signed an agreement with the small modular reactor company, Kairos Power, to supply it with nuclear energy.

AI power demand has fueled the meteoric rise of utility stocks in the past year, especially those with significant nuclear assets like Constellation, as the hyperscalers want to maintain their 'green' credentials while growing their AI businesses.

Kudos to the sector specialists at Reaves who foresaw this opportunity and bet big on the trend.

Distribution & Yield

While the UTES ETF has performed very well, it is not a typical conservative Utilities sector fund in the sense that the UTES ETF does not pay a very high distribution yield. In fact, with a 1.6% trailing 12-month yield, the UTES ETF's distribution is lower than the average ETF (Figure 9).

Conservative yield-oriented investors seeking stable income would likely have to manufacture their own distributions (i.e. sell a portion of their holdings) with the UTES ETF rather than rely on the fund.

Risks To UTES

In my opinion, the biggest risk to the UTES ETF is its AI exposure, precisely the factor that has driven its enormous returns in the past year. Sector-wide, Utility stocks' valuations are approaching 20x Fwd P/E, the upper end of historical valuation ranges dating back to the 1990s (Figure 10).

In particular, UTES' largest holdings, NEE, CEG, and VST, trade at Fwd P/E ratios above the sector average (Figure 11).

If the AI investment theme suffers a correction in the coming quarters, I expect AI-exposed utility companies like Constellation may be hit particularly hard, as it is trading at elevated valuation levels.

I personally believe the AI investment theme is a giant bubble, larger than the dot-com bubble, as NVIDIA's (NVDA) market cap is now worth 11.7% of U.S. GDP, greater than Cisco's share of U.S. GDP in 2000 (Figure 12).

However, so far, my cautions have been proven wrong, so caveat emptor.

Conclusion

The Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF is an actively managed ETF focused on the Utilities sector. The fund has performed tremendously well, returning nearly 57% in the past year, as the manager has struck gold with the AI investment theme.

However, by hitching its wagon to the AI investment theme, the manager may also expose UTES investors to above-average volatility, if the theme were to turn sour.

Personally, I am cautious about the AI investment theme and would not bet on the UTES ETF. I rate UTES a hold.

data.title: Tide Shifting To Support EM Local Currency Bonds
data.description:
Despite recent headwinds from a strong U.S. dollar, EM local currency bonds present a compelling investment opportunity due to favorable fundamentals, easing U.S. monetary policy, and new Chinese stimulus measures.

For much of the past four years, including most of this year, the strength of the U.S. dollar has been a headwind for emerging markets (EM) assets, including local currency sovereign bonds. Although the asset class has outperformed broad U.S. and global investment-grade bonds, investor sentiment has been weak based on investment flows. This is despite very favorable fundamentals relative to developed markets. With favorable growth and fiscal trends, controlled inflation and high levels of real interest rates, all on top of a significantly lower gross debt-to-GDP ratio, the case for EM local currency bonds has been strong, in our opinion. The change in the rate cycle in the U.S. and the magnitude of new Chinese stimulus measures further strengthen the case, and we believe it's time for investors to consider an allocation to EM local currency bonds within their global bond portfolio.

Easing monetary policy in the U.S. removes a headwind that has been in place since the Fed began hiking aggressively in 2022. A soft landing in the U.S., which remains a likely scenario over the next six to twelve months, provides a "goldilocks" scenario of lower rates and support for economic growth. In China, new fiscal and monetary stimulus along with new measures to stimulate the domestic property market were announced. The dynamics taking place in the world's two largest economies are positive for global growth, and many of those benefits will accrue to emerging markets. For example, commodity-sensitive currencies will likely benefit from higher growth. In that category, Latin American currencies have been the worst performers in the J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Core Index this year, providing upside potential in our opinion, particularly given that the new measures in China took the market by surprise. Latin America accounts for more than 25% of the index.

EMFX Year-to-date Performance (as of 9/30/2024)

Longer term, we expect emerging markets to continue to compare favorably overall to the U.S. and other developed markets from a fundamental perspective. U.S. government spending and borrowing trends are highly unfavorable for the U.S. dollar, in our opinion, and there is little reason to expect any changes in the current trajectory. The continued monetary and fiscal stimulus also suggests that there could be higher inflation than we had seen the decade prior to the Covid-driven shock, and that may benefit commodity producers.

EM local currency sovereign bonds are generally considered a "risk-on" asset class, despite these favorable fundamentals, which exposes the asset class to certain technical risks in the near term. Geopolitical instability, the upcoming U.S. elections and a harder landing in the U.S. than currently anticipated are the most obvious risks. However, we believe the long-term fundamentals make the asset class attractive, particularly as we move into a period of Fed easing, stimulus measures being implemented globally and a weaker or stabilized U.S. dollar.

Disclosures

Please note that VanEck may offer investments products that invest in the asset class(es) or industries included in this blog.

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

The J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Core tracks local currency bonds issued by emerging markets governments.

Investments in emerging markets bonds may be substantially more volatile, and substantially less liquid, than the bonds of governments, government agencies, and government-owned corporations located in more developed foreign markets. Emerging markets bonds can have greater custodial and operational risks, and less developed legal and accounting systems than developed markets.

All investing is subject to risk, including the possible loss of the money you invest. As with any investment strategy, there is no guarantee that investment objectives will be met and investors may lose money. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss in a declining market. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission of Van Eck Associates Corporation.

© 2024 Van Eck Associates Corporation.

Original Post
